scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 5 anti-patterns identified by name — shared database, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-as-job-runner, direct cross-service DB reads. (2) Prioritized remediation plan with 5 phases sequenced by blast radius. (3) Shared database and direct DB reads identified as highest risk (Phase 1-2 tactical fixes + Phase 2 strategic split). (4) REST-based jobs explicitly identified as fragile with no retry/DLQ/backpressure. (5) Trade-offs for shared database fix thoroughly covered — data migration, dual-write period, eventual consistency, increased network calls. (6) Incremental remediation via Strangler Fig and Expand-Contract patterns, explicitly warns against big-bang migration.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns identified with clear explanations of why each is harmful
Remediation prioritized by blast radius with explicit phasing (shared DB > notification fan-in > god endpoint > HTTP jobs > CDC for reporting)
Tactical stabilization phase (circuit breakers, read replicas, security audit) before strategic changes — shows operational maturity
Expand-Contract pattern for database split is the correct migration strategy, well-detailed with 6 steps
Trade-offs section is exceptionally thorough with alternatives_considered for each decision
Pitfalls section catches subtle risks like event bus without schema registry and deprecating endpoints without usage instrumentation

Priority ordering slightly deviates from rubric: notification fan-in is Phase 2 (short-term) while rubric places it at medium-term; HTTP jobs are Phase 5 while rubric places them at short-term. However, the overall sequencing is well-reasoned and defensible.
Security risk of publicly accessible job endpoints identified — not in the rubric but a valuable catch
Domain model with bounded contexts and context map adds architectural depth beyond what was required

agent_output_excerpt:
{
  "executive_summary": "This system exhibits five interconnected architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years: shared database coupling, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, HTTP-based background jobs, and cross-service direct database reads. Left unaddressed, these create a fragile system where any change to one service risks cascading failures across the platform.
